# Introduction {#sec-intro}

By the end of this unit, you will be able to:

-   define an EBV and explain what it is for;
-   name the six EBV classes and recognize how they appear on the portal;
-   explain why the EBVCube stores data as netCDF, and what its format adds;
-   find and inspect a dataset on the EBV Data Portal.

This unit fills in the concepts you will lean on for the rest of the tutorial: what an EBV is, why the is settled on netCDF, and how datasets are organized. You do not need any software yet. We install `ebvcube` in Unit 2. For now, everything happens in your browser, on the EBV Data portal.

## What is an Essential Biodiversity Variable?

An **Essential Biodiversity Variable (EBV)** is a standardized, derived measurement that captures a key dimension of biodiversity and enables consistent monitoring of its status and change over time. The concept was proposed by Pereira et al. (2013) and is maintained by the [Group on Earth Observations Biodiversity Observation Network (GEO BON)](https://geobon.org/). EBVs are produced by integrating and harmonizing multiple primary data sources, such as field observations, remote-sensing products, and model outputs, into a common, analysis-ready format. As a result, they provide an intermediate layer between raw biodiversity observations and the indicators used for biodiversity assessment, reporting, and decision making.

Think of an EBV as the biodiversity counterpart to an *Essential Climate Variable*: a small, agreed-upon set of variables that different research groups can produce, compare, and combine because they follow the same definitions. That shared standardization is what makes biodiversity data from different projects, taxa, and regions consistent enough to track over time.

## The six EBV classes

EBVs are organized into six classes, each capturing a different dimension of biodiversity:

-   **Genetic composition**
-   **Species populations**
-   **Species traits**
-   **Community composition**
-   **Ecosystem structure**
-   **Ecosystem function**

> The dataset we use throughout this tutorial, *Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM)*, belongs to the **Community composition** class.

::: callout-note
## Canonical classes vs. what you see on the portal

The six EBV classes above are the canonical classification defined by GEO BON. On the [EBV Data Portal](https://portal.geobon.org/datasets), you may not see all six listed at once, the portal shows only the classes for which datasets are currently available. You may also come across more specific labels such as **Species distribution** or **Ecosystem disturbance**; these are finer sub-categories within the canonical classes.

> **Ecosystem services** is not one of the six EBV classes. It relates to a separate, complementary GEO BON framework, the **Essential Ecosystem Service Variables (EESVs)**, which addresses the benefits people obtain from nature rather than biodiversity itself. For the official EBV classification, refer to the [GEO BON definitions page](https://geobon.org/ebvs/what-are-ebvs/).
:::

## Why netCDF? The EBVCube format

A single EBV has a value at every location (**longitude**, **latitude**), at several points in **time**, and for each biological **entity** measured (a species, a group, an ecosystem type). Stored as separate map images, that becomes an unwieldy pile of files with no built-in context. The [Network Common Data Form (netCDF)](https://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/), long used in the climate sciences, instead holds it all in one *self-describing* file: the data and the information that explains it units, dates, entities, projection and travel together.

The **EBVCube** is the standardized, netCDF based data format defined for EBVs. It follows two established conventions: **CF v1.8** (how coordinates, units, and time are encoded) and **ACDD v1.3** (the attributes portals use to index datasets), plus a few EBV specific rules set out in the [EBVCube format specification](https://github.com/EBVcube/EBVCube-format).

Every data cube inside an EBVCube shares the same four dimensions, think of each pixel as carrying four pieces of information: *where* (longitude, latitude), *when* (time), and *which* entity:

| Dimension | Meaning |
|:----------------|:------------------------------------------------------|
| `lon` | Longitude (or projected x-coordinate) |
| `lat` | Latitude (or projected y-coordinate) |
| `time` | Time steps (annual, decadal, scenario years, irregular) |
| `entity` | The biological unit (e.g. species, group of species, or ecosystem type) |

The `entity` dimension allows a single EBVCube to contain data for multiple species or other biological entities. Beyond these four dimensions, *metric* and *scenario* **groups** organize different biodiversity measures or future projections within the same file. We return to that structure in Unit 4.

![Overview of the EBVCube data model. An EBV dataset combines a hierarchical structure of scenarios and metrics with four-dimensional data cubes (longitude, latitude, time, and entity), and carries standardized metadata following the CF 1.8, ACDD 1.3, and EBV conventions. Source: Quoß et al. (in preparation).](images/ebvcube-structure.png){#fig-ebvcube-structure fig-alt="The EBVCube data model: a hierarchy of scenarios and metrics over four-dimensional data cubes, plus standardized metadata."}

## Touring the EBV Data Portal

All published EBV datasets are available through the [EBV Data Portal](https://portal.geobon.org/). Throughout this tutorial, we will use a single dataset as our running example:

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Worked example: [Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM)](https://portal.geobon.org/ebv-detail?id=1)

-   **Reference:** Martins, I., Pereira, H., Navarro, L. (2022). Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM) (Version 1) \[Dataset\]. German Centre for Integrative Biodiversity Research (iDiv) Halle-Jena-Leipzig. (https://doi.org/10.25829/8grx36)
-   **EBV class:** Community composition
-   **ID:** 1
-   **DOI:** [10.25829/8grx36](https://doi.org/10.25829/8grx36)
:::

Open the dataset page and you will find everything that makes an EBV dataset **FAIR** (findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable):

-   a stable **dataset ID** (`1`) and a persistent **DOI** you can cite;
-   a structured **JSON metadata record** (title, abstract, EBV class, creators, license, entities);
-   the **netCDF file** itself, available for direct download (≈ 4 MB);
-   an interactive **EBV Visualizer** for exploring maps and temporal trends without downloading anything.

The three views you will use most are the *Discover* page (@fig-portal-discover), an individual *dataset page* (@fig-portal-detail), and the live *EBV Visualizer* below. Click either screenshot to enlarge it.

::: panel-tabset
### Discover page

![The *discover* page showing the EBV class filters and a list of datasets.](images/portal-discover.png){#fig-portal-discover fig-alt="EBV Data Portal Discover page showing EBV class filters and a list of datasets."}

### Dataset page

![The dataset page for id = 1, showing its gerneral metadata, and the netCDF download button.](images/portal-detail.png){#fig-portal-detail fig-alt="Detail page for the Local bird diversity dataset with metadata and a download button."}

### EBV Visualizer

<iframe src="https://portal.geobon.org/map/?id=1" width="100%" height="600" style="border:1px solid #ddd; border-radius:6px;" loading="lazy">

</iframe>
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Portal interface as of July 2026. The portal is updated from time to time, so small layout differences are normal, the metadata, DOI, and download are always on the dataset page.
:::

Browsing and inspecting datasets like this is how you *find* data on the portal. Next, we look at how you *work with* it.

## Where `ebvcube` fits

The portal is where EBV data is *published and discovered*; the **`ebvcube`** R package is how you *work with it*. It sits on both sides of the portal:

- **Reading published data.** Give `ebvcube` a dataset's numeric ID and it downloads the file, lists the data cubes inside, and reads, subsets, or maps them for you, pulling dataset id = 1 into R takes a couple of lines instead of a manual download and netCDF wrangling.

- **Producing new data.** Starting from your own rasters or model output, `ebvcube` assembles them into a valid EBVCube, ready to upload and publish on the portal with its own DOI.

Read on one side, create on the other: that round trip is the workflow in the Overview diagram, and the path the rest of this tutorial follows, one step at a time.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Checkpoint.** You just inspected dataset id = 1 on the portal, its **EBV class** (Community composition), its **DOI**, and its **file format** (netCDF). If those three make sense, you are ready to install the tools in Unit 2.
:::
